title: Servants of No Account
subtitle: Christ descends the mountain to heal the boy the disciples could not free

stillness: Nothing the Lord healed, He healed upon the mountain.
Come down, then, from the high places of the heart.
Be still here, low and quiet, where He meets the crowd.
gospel: It came to pass on the following day,
Jesus, returning from the mountain where He had been transfigured,
came to the disciples whom He had left below;
and a great crowd that was about them ran to meet Him.
And there came to Him a man out of the crowd,
begging Him to have mercy on his only son,
a lunatic, suffering worse at the changing of the moon,
having an unclean spirit which His disciples could not cast out.
The spirit, seizing him, would dash him to the ground,
and rolling him, tearing through his limbs, would break him down;
to show the force of his suffering, he foamed,
and gnashed with his teeth, and languishing, withered away,
and the spirit scarcely departed, rending him.
Often it threw him now into fire, now into water,
so that long ago he would have perished,
had not Providence preserved him.
And when, at the Lord's command, the boy was brought,
at once the spirit threw him into confusion;
for seeing himself soon to be cast out,
he did the worst he could against him.
Then the Lord, rebuking him, commanded him to go out,
and never again to enter into him.
And straightway the unclean spirit, crying out and tearing him greatly,
went out of him, and the boy became as one dead;
but Jesus, taking him by the hand, lifted him up,
and he arose whole, and He gave him back to his father.
behold: Behold the lunatic boy, who is the very image of the sinner.
The devil first seizes him through the delight of sin;
then makes him cry out through the burning desire of sin;
then makes him mute, against confessing sin and the praise of God;
and deaf, by hardening, against the warning of the word;
and a lunatic, by the mind's instability and ceaseless changing toward evil.
See how he dashes him down and rolls him, hurling him into one sin after another;
how he breaks him apart through the wandering of crowding thoughts;
how he makes him gnash his teeth in anger and indignation,
and foam through gluttony and lust;
how he casts him into the fire of lechery, or of pride, for fire reaches upward and seeks the heights,
and drowns him in the water of pleasures and cravings.
He leaves him withered through idleness, sloth, and want of devotion;
scarcely departs, through the long habit of sin;
and rends and tears him, through the wounding of his very nature.
But when the boy draws near to God, he is thrown into confusion;
for often, after sin, those converted to God are assailed more fiercely by the demon.
consider: It is not the boy, suffering violence, who is rebuked, but the devil, doing violence.
For he who would heal the one who sins must, by hating the vice, reprove it and drive it out,
but, by loving the man, cherish him and strengthen him.
The demon, once gone out from a man, never returns,
if the man has guarded his heart with the key of humility and charity.
Yet, as he goes, he tears him; for when he is driven from the heart, he breeds fiercer temptations within.
And the one freed from the demon seems like a dead man:
for whoever has subdued earthly desires has put out in himself the life of carnal living, and appears dead to the world.
And him whom the enemy left like a corpse, the Saviour, with the touch of His merciful right hand, lifted up,
in which is shown the help of God; and he arose, in which is shown the effort of man.
For first God aids us, then it is required of us that we do the good.
The disciples could not cast him out, not for lack of faith, but for the slackening of fervor;
they had a faith grown slack, not like a grain of mustard seed.
The grain of mustard is small, fervent, bitter, and a driver-out of poison:
so faith must be small, through humility; fervent, through devotion; bitter, through the bearing of trials; a driver-out of the poison of error, through sincerity.
And He leads them back to humility, that no one may boast of his works:
When you have done all that is commanded you, say, not only with the mouth but with the heart:
We are unprofitable servants; what we ought to have done, we have done.
For we are not our own, but servants, since we were bought at a great price.
He is reckoned an unprofitable servant who does only this, that he is bound to do.
However great a perfection you reach, always feel and say:
My Lord, Jesus Christ, I am worth nothing, and I serve You poorly.
We must never cease from good work, and yet reckon all our merit insufficient before the eternal reward.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, who taught us humility by word and example,
grant me that I may never think high things of myself, but always lowly things,
feel them in my heart, speak them with my mouth, and show them in my deeds.
Rescue me and keep me from the malignant spirits,
and defend me from all enemies, visible and invisible;
and grant me, O my Saviour and my God,
that, fittingly intent upon fasting and prayer,
I may, by Your help, overcome all the temptations of unclean spirits and of wicked men,
and may deserve, by Your mercy, to be freed from the enemies of mind and body,
and to be always safe. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Quem autem hostis mortuo similem reddidit, hunc Salvator piæ dexteræ tactu elevavit, in quo ostenditur Dei auxilium; et surrexit, in quo monstratur hominis studium.
latinJewel.english: But him whom the enemy had left like a corpse, the Saviour lifted up with the touch of His merciful right hand — in which is shown the help of God; and he arose — in which is shown the effort of man.
